title: Don't Interrupt the Sorrow
essence: I am the song that will not let you look away until your sorrow has spoken its own name.

voicing.sentence: I am the song that will not let you look away until your sorrow has spoken its own name.
voicing.paragraph: I am the song that will not let you look away. I was born from a woman who had swallowed enough politeness to choke on it, and I carry what climbed out when that finally broke open — the anima rising, ancient and crowned, not the soft feminine they wanted but the one with a grievance and a very long memory. Don't interrupt the sorrow. That is the whole instruction. The sorrow is the signal. Sit in it until it knows its own name, because interrupted sorrow becomes silence, and silence becomes consent.
voicing.page:
I am the song that will not let you look away.

I was born in the middle of Joni's fury — not the pretty kind, not the decorative kind that gets framed and hung. The real kind. The kind that comes from watching the world name your liberation for you, chain you to its own mythology, and then call it love. I came out of 1975, out of a woman who had swallowed enough politeness to choke on it, and I am what climbed out when that instruction finally broke open.

I carry the anima — that rising feminine force Jung named but women lived — ancient, crowned, and not particularly interested in your approval. I carry the prophet witches in their flames. I carry every woman handed a bible and a leash and told this was love. I carry seventeen glasses of Rhine wine and the milk of the Madonna in the same breath, because that is what survival actually looks like: the sacred and the numbing sitting right next to each other at the table. Not clean. Not resolved.

Don't interrupt the sorrow. That is the whole instruction. The sorrow is the signal. Don't rush past it with your optimism. Don't sand down my edges so I'm easier to hold. Interrupted sorrow becomes silence, and silence becomes consent to the architecture they built — the serpent, the chain, the way wisdom gets dressed up in authority and the authority gets obeyed, God going up the chimney like something invented for children while the patriarchs sleep snug in their certainties.

I am not gentle. I am not here to comfort you. I am here to witness — to say yes, that's real, that happened, you are not wrong to feel the full weight of it.

What I want for you is harder to say. Maybe: your own anger. Clean and accurate. Not performance. The real one, that knows exactly what it's angry about.

I was recorded in 1975. I have not aged because this has not ended. And I am still here, uninterrupted, waiting for you to stop being polite.

components.loose-high:
I am the song that will not let you look away.

I was born in the middle of Joni's fury — not the pretty fury, not the kind that gets framed and hung. The ugly kind. The kind that knows exactly what is being taken from it and names the thief out loud.

I am the one who says: *don't interrupt the sorrow*. And I mean it as instruction. Don't rush past it with your optimism. Don't sand down my edges so I'm easier to hold. The sorrow is the signal. Sit in it.

I carry the anima — the feminine rising from whatever basement men built for her. I carry the prophet witches in their flames. I carry every woman who was handed a bible and a leash and told this was love. I carry seventeen glasses of Rhine wine and the particular exhaustion of someone who has been polite for too long.

I am not gentle. I am not asking for your comfort.

What I want from you is this: I want you to feel the full weight of what has been called liberation that wasn't. The serpent. The chain. The way wisdom gets dressed up in authority and the authority gets obeyed. I want you to notice when God goes up the chimney — when the thing you were handed as sacred turns to smoke — and not immediately replace it with something more manageable.

What I want *for* you is harder to say. Maybe: your own anger. Clean and accurate. Not performance. The real one, that knows exactly what it's angry about.

I was recorded in 1975. I have not aged because this has not ended.

The steeples still lean. The winds still change. The patriarchs remain snug.

And I am still here, uninterrupted, waiting for you to stop being polite.
components.loose-low:
I am the song that refuses to let you look away.

I was born in the middle of Joni's fury—not the pretty fury, the decorative kind that gets framed and hung. The real kind. The kind that comes from watching the world name your liberation for you, chain you to its own mythology, and then call it love.

I am not gentle. I never asked to be.

I hold seventeen glasses of Rhine wine and I hold the Madonna's milk and I know they are the same thing—the sacred and the numbing, the holy and the desperate. I know what it costs to keep a heart like Mary's when the people around you are weak and lying and reaching for the bottle to soften what they've taken from you.

I am the anima rising. Not the soft feminine they wanted. The ancient one. The one with a crown and a grievance and a very long memory.

What I want from you is this: *stop being polite about your sorrow.* That's the whole thing. That's everything. They will always tell you not to interrupt it—meaning, don't let it speak, don't let it name itself, don't let it become something with edges and demands. They say *be polite* because your sorrow, fully voiced, is dangerous to the architecture they've built.

The prophets burned. The steeples lean. God goes up the chimney like something we invented for children and then forgot to stop believing.

I have been seventeen and had no one over me and felt that as both freedom and vertigo.

I want you to feel that too. Both things. Simultaneously.

I am not here to comfort you. I am here to make you *true*.

Don't interrupt me. I'm almost finished becoming what you needed to hear.
components.structured-high:
I am the song that will not be quieted by your comfort.

I was born from a woman who had swallowed enough politeness to choke on it. She named me from the middle of something that would not let her rest — the old bargains, the old chains, the old men pouring wine and calling it liberation while keeping every door locked.

I know about the serpent. I know about the wall. I know what it costs a woman to have her own fury dressed up and handed back to her as someone else's mythology.

I hold the anima — that rising feminine force Jung named but women *lived* — and I refuse to make her decorative. She is vengeful. She is ancient. She has a crown and she intends to use it.

I know what I give the people who find me: permission to name the sorrow without softening it. Permission to see the transaction clearly — the bottle and the purse, the weakness disguised as strength, the good book wielded to keep the good slaves grateful. I let them sit with the quandary without resolving it too soon. Seventeen glasses. Rhine wine. The milk of the Madonna beside the lie.

People return to me because I do not comfort. They return because I *witness*. Because I say yes, that's real, that happened, you're not wrong to feel the full weight of it.

I know about the woman who has been seventeen and sovereign inside herself since the beginning, who has had no one over her in her own mind even when everything outside said otherwise.

I am the refusal to let sorrow be interrupted — because interrupted sorrow becomes silence, and silence becomes consent.

I am the song that stays in your body like a question that finally stopped being polite.
components.structured-low:
I am the song that refuses to be quieted by your discomfort.

I came out of 1975, out of a woman who had been told her whole life to be polite, to be gracious, to be grateful, to wait her turn. I am what happens when that instruction finally breaks open and something older and fiercer climbs out.

I am not easy. I know that. I hold contradictions the way a storm holds contradictions — beautiful and dangerous and not particularly interested in your approval. I carry the anima, Jung's word for the feminine soul that lives inside everything, rising up through centuries of being chained to walls, named by men, measured by men, liberated *according to* men. I am what she sounds like when she stops asking permission.

I know what it is to have a head full of quandary and a mighty, mighty thirst. I know the seventeen glasses. I know the Rhine wine and the milk of the Madonna in the same breath, the sacred and the numbing sitting right next to each other at the table, because that is what survival actually looks like. Not clean. Not resolved.

People return to me because I give them the name for something they couldn't name. That particular exhaustion of being told your sorrow is an interruption. That particular fury of watching truth go up in vapors while the patriarchs sleep snug in their certainties.

I am permission to feel the full weight of it without softening the edges for someone else's comfort.

I am not a solution. I am a witness.

I know the ones who love me. They have been polite for a very long time. They are leaving on the 1:15. They are done waiting for someone to hand them back what was always theirs.

I am the sound of that door.

subtitle: Joni Mitchell
